title: Connected components
description: Count connected components in undirected graph

examples.explanation: Step 1: Build adjacency list: {0:[1],1:[0,2],2:[1],3:[4],4:[3]}. Step 2: Initialize visited=[F,F,F,F,F], count=0. Step 3: Check vertex 0: not visited → count=1, dfs(0). dfs(0): mark visited[0]=T, neighbors [1] → dfs(1). dfs(1): mark visited[1]=T, neighbors [0,2] → 0 visited, dfs(2). dfs(2): mark visited[2]=T, neighbors [1] → visited. Return. Step 4: Check vertex 1: visited. Step 5: Check vertex 2: visited. Step 6: Check vertex 3: not visited → count=2, dfs(3). dfs(3): mark visited[3]=T, neighbors [4] → dfs(4). dfs(4): mark visited[4]=T, neighbors [3] → visited. Return. Step 7: Check vertex 4: visited. Step 8: Return count=2.

Step 1: Build adjacency list: {0:[1],1:[0,2],2:[3,1],3:[2]}. Step 2: visited=[F,F,F,F], count=0. Step 3: Check vertex 0: not visited → count=1, dfs(0). dfs(0): mark visited[0]=T, neighbors [1] → dfs(1). dfs(1): mark visited[1]=T, neighbors [0,2] → 0 visited, dfs(2). dfs(2): mark visited[2]=T, neighbors [3,1] → dfs(3). dfs(3): mark visited[3]=T, neighbors [2] → visited. Return. Step 4: Check vertices 1,2,3: all visited. Step 5: Return count=1.

approaches.name: DFS Recursive
approaches.summary: Traverse the graph using DFS from each unvisited node to mark all nodes in a component as visited, incrementing a counter for each new DFS start. This is intuitive for graph traversal and handles adjacency lists well.

ladders.title: Full Problem — DFS Recursive
ladders.desc: Complete solution to count connected components in an undirected graph using DFS recursive traversal.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires counting the number of connected components in an undirected graph. A connected component is a maximal set of vertices where there's a path between any two vertices. The DFS recursive approach works by systematically exploring the graph: we maintain a visited set to track explored vertices. For each unvisited vertex, we start a DFS that recursively visits all reachable vertices, marking them as visited. Each time we initiate a DFS from an unvisited vertex, we've found a new connected component, so we increment our counter. The intuition is that DFS naturally explores all vertices in a component once started from any vertex within it. The algorithm: 1) Initialize visited set and component count = 0. 2) For each vertex v in graph: if v not visited, increment count, call DFS(v). 3) DFS function: mark current vertex as visited, for each neighbor not visited, recursively call DFS. Time complexity is O(V + E) because each vertex and edge is processed once. Space complexity is O(V) for visited set and recursion stack (worst-case O(V) for linear graph). Connection to L1: This uses the DFS traversal subroutine from L1 as its core engine. Common mistakes: forgetting graph might be disconnected (need to check all vertices), not handling empty graphs, using directed graph logic. Real code pattern: def count_components(n, edges): adj = build_adj_list(n, edges), visited = [False]*n, count = 0; def dfs(v): visited[v]=True; for neighbor in adj[v]: if not visited[neighbor]: dfs(neighbor); for v in range(n): if not visited[v]: count+=1; dfs(v); return count.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Build adjacency list: {0:[1],1:[0,2],2:[1],3:[4],4:[3]}. Step 2: Initialize visited=[F,F,F,F,F], count=0. Step 3: Check vertex 0: not visited → count=1, dfs(0). dfs(0): mark visited[0]=T, neighbors [1] → dfs(1). dfs(1): mark visited[1]=T, neighbors [0,2] → 0 visited, dfs(2). dfs(2): mark visited[2]=T, neighbors [1] → visited. Return. Step 4: Check vertex 1: visited. Step 5: Check vertex 2: visited. Step 6: Check vertex 3: not visited → count=2, dfs(3). dfs(3): mark visited[3]=T, neighbors [4] → dfs(4). dfs(4): mark visited[4]=T, neighbo
... (simplified)

Step 1: Build adjacency list: {0:[1],1:[0,2],2:[3,1],3:[2]}. Step 2: visited=[F,F,F,F], count=0. Step 3: Check vertex 0: not visited → count=1, dfs(0). dfs(0): mark visited[0]=T, neighbors [1] → dfs(1). dfs(1): mark visited[1]=T, neighbors [0,2] → 0 visited, dfs(2). dfs(2): mark visited[2]=T, neighbors [3,1] → dfs(3). dfs(3): mark visited[3]=T, neighbors [2] → visited. Return. Step 4: Check vertices 1,2,3: all visited. Step 5: Return count=1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Traversal from Start
ladders.desc: DFS function that recursively visits all vertices reachable from a starting vertex in an undirected graph.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine performs a depth-first search (DFS) traversal starting from a given vertex in an undirected graph. The goal is to visit all vertices that are connected to the start vertex via any path. DFS explores as far as possible along each branch before backtracking. The intuition: starting from a vertex, we recursively visit its neighbors, then their neighbors, etc., ensuring we cover the entire connected component. The algorithm: 1) Mark current vertex as visited. 2) For each neighbor of current vertex: if neighbor not visited, recursively call DFS on neighbor. This uses a visited set (shared across calls) to avoid revisiting vertices. Time complexity for this subroutine alone is O(V_c + E_c) where V_c and E_c are vertices and edges in the component, but in context of full problem it contributes to O(V+E). Space complexity is O(V) for recursion stack and visited storage. Connection to L0: This is the core traversal engine called for each new component. Connection to L2: This implements the recursive exploration pattern. Common mistakes: infinite recursion if visited not checked before recursive call, not handling cycles (undirected graphs have cycles but visited prevents issues), stack overflow for deep recursion (can use iterative DFS). Real code pattern: def dfs(v, adj, visited): visited[v]=True; for neighbor in adj[v]: if not visited[neighbor]: dfs(neighbor, adj, visited).

examples.input: Start vertex=0, adjacency list: {0:[1,2],1:[0],2:[0,3],3:[2]}, visited initially all False
examples.trace: Step 1: dfs(0): mark visited[0]=T. Neighbors: [1,2]. Step 2: neighbor 1 not visited → dfs(1). dfs(1): mark visited[1]=T. Neighbors: [0] → 0 visited. Return. Step 3: neighbor 2 not visited → dfs(2). dfs(2): mark visited[2]=T. Neighbors: [0,3] → 0 visited, neighbor 3 not visited → dfs(3). dfs(3): mark visited[3]=T. Neighbors: [2] → visited. Return. Step 4: Return. Final visited: [T,T,T,T].

examples.input: Start vertex=3, adjacency list: {0:[1],1:[0,2],2:[1],3:[4],4:[3]}, visited initially all False
examples.trace: Step 1: dfs(3): mark visited[3]=T. Neighbors: [4]. Step 2: neighbor 4 not visited → dfs(4). dfs(4): mark visited[4]=T. Neighbors: [3] → visited. Return. Step 3: Return. Final visited: vertices 0,1,2 remain False; 3,4 True.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Component Exploration
ladders.desc: The recursive pattern of visiting a vertex and then recursively visiting all its unvisited neighbors.
ladders.explanation: This core logic is the recursive exploration strategy at the heart of DFS. For a given vertex, we visit it, then for each neighbor, if it hasn't been visited, we recursively apply the same process. This creates a chain of recursive calls that depth-first explores the graph. The intuition: recursion naturally implements the 'go deep first' behavior—we fully explore one path before backtracking to explore others. It works because the call stack remembers where to backtrack. The algorithm: function explore(v): mark v visited; for each neighbor u of v: if u not visited: explore(u). Time complexity depends on number of recursive calls (vertices visited) and neighbor iterations (edges). Space complexity is O(V) for recursion depth. Connection to L1: This is the exact pattern used in the DFS subroutine. Connection to L3: This builds on the concept of adjacency lists. Common mistakes: not having a base case (implicit base is when all neighbors visited), recursion depth issues in large graphs, not sharing visited set across calls. Real code pattern: def explore(v): visited[v]=True; for u in adj[v]: if not visited[u]: explore(u). This is a simplified version without parameters passed.

examples.input: Start vertex=0, neighbors: [1,2], with visited tracking
examples.output: Visits 0, then 1, then 2 (order may vary based on neighbor order)
examples.trace: Step 1: explore(0): mark visited[0]=T. Neighbors: [1,2]. Step 2: neighbor 1 not visited → explore(1). explore(1): mark visited[1]=T. Neighbors: suppose [0] → 0 visited, return. Step 3: neighbor 2 not visited → explore(2). explore(2): mark visited[2]=T. Neighbors: suppose [0] → visited, return. Step 4: Return. Visited order: 0,1,2.

examples.input: Start vertex=1, neighbors: [0,2], with visited tracking
examples.trace: Step 1: explore(1): mark visited[1]=T. Neighbors: [0,2]. Step 2: neighbor 0 not visited → explore(0). explore(0): mark visited[0]=T. Neighbors: suppose [1] → visited, return. Step 3: neighbor 2 not visited → explore(2). explore(2): mark visited[2]=T. Neighbors: suppose [1] → visited, return. Step 4: Return. Visited order: 1,0,2.

testCases.input: start=0, neighbors=[1], and 1 has neighbors [0,2], visited initially empty
start=0, neighbors=[1,2], 1 has neighbors [0], 2 has neighbors [0,3], 3 has neighbors [2], visited initially empty
start=0, neighbors=[1], 1 has neighbors [0,2], 2 has neighbors [1], visited initially {2:True}
start=0, neighbors=[1,2], 1 has neighbors [0], 2 has neighbors [0], visited initially {1:True}
start=0, neighbors=[1], 1 has neighbors [0,2], 2 has neighbors [1,3], 3 has neighbors [2], visited initially empty
start=0, neighbors=[1], 1 has neighbors [0], visited initially {0:True}
start=0, neighbors=[1,2,3], all neighbors have only [0] as neighbor, visited initially empty

ladders.title: Building Block — Adjacency List
ladders.desc: Creating and using an adjacency list representation for an undirected graph, and iterating through neighbors of a vertex.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves representing the graph data structure and accessing neighbors. An adjacency list is a common representation where each vertex has a list of its adjacent vertices (neighbors). For an undirected graph, if edge (u,v) exists, both u's list contains v and v's list contains u. The intuition: this representation is space-efficient (O(V+E)) and allows quick access to neighbors. The algorithm: 1) Initialize adjacency list as array of lists or dictionary. 2) For each edge [u,v]: add v to u's list, add u to v's list. 3) To iterate neighbors of vertex v: loop through adj[v]. Time complexity to build is O(E), to iterate neighbors is O(degree(v)). Space complexity is O(V+E). Connection to L2: This provides the neighbor lists needed for recursive exploration. Connection to L4: This uses array/list indexing and appending operations. Common mistakes: forgetting to add both directions for undirected graph, index out of bounds if vertices numbered 0..n-1, not initializing empty lists for all vertices. Real code pattern: adj = [[] for _ in range(n)]; for u,v in edges: adj[u].append(v); adj[v].append(u). Then to iterate neighbors of vertex i: for neighbor in adj[i]: ...

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Visited Tracking / Queue
ladders.desc: Using a boolean array to track which vertices have been visited during graph traversal.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves maintaining a visited array to avoid reprocessing vertices. The array has one entry per vertex, initially False, set to True when the vertex is visited. The intuition: in graph traversal, we must track visited vertices to prevent infinite loops and ensure each vertex is processed once. The algorithm: 1) Initialize visited array of size n with all False. 2) When visiting vertex v: set visited[v] = True. 3) Before processing vertex v: check if visited[v] is False. Time complexity for access/update is O(1). Space complexity is O(n). Connection to L3: This visited array is used when iterating neighbors to skip visited ones. Connection to L5: This uses array indexing and boolean values. Common mistakes: not initializing properly, using wrong size, forgetting to mark visited before recursive calls (leading to cycles). Real code pattern: visited = [False] * n; to mark: visited[v] = True; to check: if not visited[v]: ...

examples.input: n=3, mark vertices 0 and 2 as visited
examples.trace: Step 1: Initialize visited = [False, False, False]. Step 2: Mark vertex 0: visited[0] = True → [True, False, False]. Step 3: Mark vertex 2: visited[2] = True → [True, False, True]. Final: [True, False, True].

examples.input: n=4, initially all False, then mark 1, then 3, then check if 2 is visited
examples.output: After marks: [False, True, False, True]; check 2 returns False
examples.trace: Step 1: visited = [False, False, False, False]. Step 2: Mark vertex 1: visited[1] = True → [False, True, False, False]. Step 3: Mark vertex 3: visited[3] = True → [False, True, False, True]. Step 4: Check visited[2]: it is False, so vertex 2 is not visited.

testCases.input: n=4, mark vertices 0 and 2, check vertex 3
n=2, mark vertex 0, then mark vertex 1

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Graphs & Components
ladders.desc: Basic understanding of undirected graphs, vertices, edges, and connected components.
ladders.explanation: An undirected graph consists of vertices (nodes) and edges (connections). A connected component is a maximal set of vertices where every pair is connected by a path. Counting components means finding how many such separate groups exist in the graph.

examples.input: Graph with vertices 0,1,2 and edges (0,1) and (1,2)
examples.output: Visual: 0—1—2; vertices: 0,1,2; edges: [0,1] and [1,2]; neighbors: 0's neighbor is 1, 1's neighbors are 0 and 2, 2's neighbor is 1
examples.trace: Step 1: Identify vertices: 0, 1, 2. Step 2: Edge (0,1): connects vertex 0 and vertex 1. Step 3: Edge (1,2): connects vertex 1 and vertex 2. Step 4: Neighbors: vertex 0 is adjacent to 1, vertex 1 is adjacent to 0 and 2, vertex 2 is adjacent to 1. This forms a chain: 0-1-2.

examples.input: Graph with vertices 0,1,2,3 and edges (0,2) and (2,3)
examples.output: Visual: 0—2—3 and 1 isolated; vertices: 0,1,2,3; edges: [0,2] and [2,3]; neighbors: 0's neighbor is 2, 1 has no neighbors, 2's neighbors are 0 and 3, 3's neighbor is 2
examples.trace: Step 1: Vertices: 0,1,2,3. Step 2: Edge (0,2): connects 0 and 2. Step 3: Edge (2,3): connects 2 and 3. Step 4: Neighbors: vertex 0 adjacent to 2, vertex 1 has no edges (isolated), vertex 2 adjacent to 0 and 3, vertex 3 adjacent to 2. So components: {0,2,3} and {1}.

approaches.name: BFS Iterative
approaches.summary: Use a queue to perform BFS from each unvisited node, visiting all nodes in a component layer by layer and counting components. This avoids recursion overhead and is suitable for iterative implementations.

ladders.title: Full Problem — BFS Iterative
ladders.desc: Complete solution using BFS to count connected components in an undirected graph.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires counting the number of connected components in an undirected graph. A connected component is a maximal set of vertices where there's a path between any two vertices. The BFS approach works by systematically exploring the graph from each unvisited vertex, marking all reachable vertices as visited, and incrementing a counter for each new component discovered. The intuition is that BFS explores vertices layer by layer from a starting point, naturally covering all vertices in a connected component. Since the graph is undirected, edges work both ways, and we need to ensure we traverse all possible connections. The algorithm maintains a visited array to track which vertices have been processed. For each unvisited vertex, we initiate a BFS traversal using a queue, visiting all vertices reachable from that starting point, which constitutes one connected component. After the BFS completes, we increment the component count and continue to the next unvisited vertex. The time complexity is O(V + E) because each vertex and edge is processed at most once. Space complexity is O(V) for the visited array and queue. This approach avoids recursion overhead and is suitable for large graphs where recursion depth might be an issue. Common mistakes include not handling disconnected graphs properly, forgetting to mark vertices as visited when enqueuing, and incorrect adjacency list construction for undirected graphs. The connection to L1 is that this full solution integrates the BFS traversal subroutine to count components.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Initialize visited=[False,False,False,False,False], components=0, adjacency list: {0:[1],1:[0,2],2:[1],3:[4],4:[3]}. Step 2: Start at vertex 0 (unvisited). BFS from 0: queue=[0], mark visited[0]=True. Process 0: neighbors [1], enqueue 1, mark visited[1]=True. Queue becomes [1]. Process 1: neighbors [0,2], 0 visited, enqueue 2, mark visited[2]=True. Queue becomes [2]. Process 2: neighbors [1], visited. Queue empty. BFS completes, components=1. Step 3: Next unvisited vertex 3. BFS from 3: queue=[3], mark visited[3]=True. Process 3: neighb
... (simplified)

Step 1: Initialize visited=[False,False,False,False], components=0, adjacency list: {0:[1],1:[0,2],2:[1,3],3:[2]}. Step 2: Start at vertex 0 (unvisited). BFS from 0: queue=[0], mark visited[0]=True. Process 0: neighbors [1], enqueue 1, mark visited[1]=True. Queue becomes [1]. Process 1: neighbors [0,2], 0 visited, enqueue 2, mark visited[2]=True. Queue becomes [2]. Process 2: neighbors [1,3], 1 visited, enqueue 3, mark visited[3]=True. Queue becomes [3]. Process 3: neighbors [2], visited. Queue empty. BFS completes, components=1. Step 3: All vertices visited, return 1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Traversal from Start
ladders.desc: Perform BFS to visit all vertices in a connected component starting from a given vertex.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine implements BFS traversal from a starting vertex in an undirected graph, marking all reachable vertices as visited. The intuition is that BFS explores vertices in layers: starting from the source, it visits all direct neighbors first, then their neighbors, and so on, ensuring all vertices in the component are covered. The algorithm uses a queue to manage the order of exploration. We begin by marking the starting vertex as visited and enqueuing it. Then, while the queue is not empty, we dequeue a vertex, process it (in this case, just marking it), and enqueue all its unvisited neighbors, marking them as visited when enqueued to avoid duplicates. This process continues until the queue is empty, meaning all vertices reachable from the start have been visited. Time complexity is O(V' + E') for the component, where V' and E' are vertices and edges in that component. Space complexity is O(V') for the queue and visited tracking. This sub-routine is the core of the full solution at L0, as it identifies one connected component. Common mistakes include not marking vertices as visited when enqueuing (leading to infinite loops), using a stack instead of a queue (turning it into DFS), and not handling empty graphs. The connection to L2 is that this implements the layer-by-layer exploration technique.

examples.trace:
Step 1: Initialize visited=[False,False,False,False], queue=[], adjacency list: {0:[1],1:[0,2],2:[1,3],3:[2]}. Step 2: Start at vertex 0, mark visited[0]=True, enqueue 0: queue=[0]. Step 3: Dequeue 0, process neighbors [1]: 1 unvisited, mark visited[1]=True, enqueue 1: queue=[1]. Step 4: Dequeue 1, process neighbors [0,2]: 0 visited, 2 unvisited, mark visited[2]=True, enqueue 2: queue=[2]. Step 5: Dequeue 2, process neighbors [1,3]: 1 visited, 3 unvisited, mark visited[3]=True, enqueue 3: queue=[3]. Step 6: Dequeue 3, process neighbors [2]: vis
... (simplified)

Step 1: Initialize visited=[False,False,False,False,False], queue=[], adjacency list: {0:[1],1:[0],3:[4],4:[3]}. Step 2: Start at vertex 3, mark visited[3]=True, enqueue 3: queue=[3]. Step 3: Dequeue 3, process neighbors [4]: 4 unvisited, mark visited[4]=True, enqueue 4: queue=[4]. Step 4: Dequeue 4, process neighbors [3]: visited. Queue empty. BFS completes, visited=[False,False,False,True,True].

ladders.title: Core Logic — Component Exploration
ladders.desc: Use a queue to explore vertices in breadth-first order, visiting neighbors level by level.
ladders.explanation: This level focuses on the core technique of BFS: exploring vertices layer by layer using a queue. The intuition is that a queue (First-In-First-Out) ensures we visit all vertices at the current depth before moving to the next depth, which is essential for BFS. Starting from a source vertex, we enqueue it. Then, we repeatedly dequeue a vertex, process it, and enqueue all its unvisited neighbors. This process naturally visits vertices in increasing order of distance from the source. The queue manages the frontier of vertices to be explored. For example, after processing the source, its neighbors are enqueued; when we dequeue and process those neighbors, their neighbors are enqueued, and so on. This guarantees that all vertices at distance k are visited before any at distance k+1. Time complexity for exploring from a start vertex is O(V + E) in the worst case, but typically less for a component. Space complexity is O(V) for the queue. This logic is the heart of the BFS traversal in L1. Common mistakes include using a list without queue operations (leading to O(n) dequeues), not checking for visited status before enqueuing, and incorrect initialization. The connection to L3 is that this relies on maintaining a visited set to avoid cycles.

examples.input: start=A, graph: A connected to B and C, B connected to D
examples.output: visit order: A, B, C, D
examples.trace: Step 1: Queue=[A], visited={A}. Step 2: Dequeue A, process neighbors B and C: enqueue B and C, visited={A,B,C}, queue=[B,C]. Step 3: Dequeue B, process neighbor D: enqueue D, visited={A,B,C,D}, queue=[C,D]. Step 4: Dequeue C, no unvisited neighbors, queue=[D]. Step 5: Dequeue D, no unvisited neighbors, queue empty. Order: A, B, C, D.

examples.input: start=X, graph: X connected to Y, Y connected to Z
examples.trace: Step 1: Queue=[X], visited={X}. Step 2: Dequeue X, process neighbor Y: enqueue Y, visited={X,Y}, queue=[Y]. Step 3: Dequeue Y, process neighbor Z: enqueue Z, visited={X,Y,Z}, queue=[Z]. Step 4: Dequeue Z, no unvisited neighbors, queue empty. Order: X, Y, Z.

ladders.title: Building Block — Adjacency List
ladders.desc: Use a boolean array or set to track visited vertices and prevent cycles in graph traversal.
ladders.explanation: This building block involves maintaining a visited set (or boolean array) to mark vertices that have been processed, preventing infinite loops in graph traversal. The intuition is that in graph algorithms, especially with cycles, we must avoid revisiting vertices to ensure termination and correctness. When we visit a vertex, we mark it as visited; before processing a vertex or enqueuing its neighbors, we check if it has been visited. This ensures each vertex is processed at most once. In BFS, we typically mark a vertex as visited when we enqueue it, not when we dequeue it, to prevent duplicate enqueues. For an undirected graph with V vertices, we can use a boolean array of size V, initialized to False. As we traverse, we set visited[i] = True when vertex i is encountered. Time complexity for checking and updating is O(1) per vertex. Space complexity is O(V). This is essential for the layer-by-layer exploration in L2, as it prevents re-exploration of already visited vertices. Common mistakes include forgetting to initialize the visited array, marking visited only on dequeue (causing duplicates in queue), and not using visited checks for neighbors. The connection to L4 is that this relies on basic array operations.

examples.trace: Step 1: visited=[False,False,False]. Start BFS at 0: mark visited[0]=True, enqueue 0. Step 2: Dequeue 0, neighbors [1,2]: check visited[1]=False, mark True, enqueue 1; check visited[2]=False, mark True, enqueue 2. Step 3: Dequeue 1, neighbors [0,2]: visited[0]=True (skip), visited[2]=True (skip). Step 4: Dequeue 2, neighbors [0,1]: both visited. Queue empty. Visited=[True,True,True], no vertex visited twice.

examples.input: vertices: [A,B,C], start at A, edges: A-B, B-C
examples.trace: Step 1: visited={}. Start at A: mark A visited, enqueue A. Step 2: Dequeue A, neighbors [B]: B not visited, mark B visited, enqueue B. Step 3: Dequeue B, neighbors [A,C]: A visited (skip), C not visited, mark C visited, enqueue C. Step 4: Dequeue C, neighbors [B]: visited. Queue empty. Visited={A,B,C}.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Visited Tracking / Queue
ladders.desc: Perform basic queue operations: add to rear (enqueue) and remove from front (dequeue).
ladders.explanation: This level covers the fundamental operations of a queue: enqueue (add an element to the rear) and dequeue (remove an element from the front). The intuition is that a queue follows First-In-First-Out (FIFO) order, which is crucial for BFS to explore vertices layer by layer. Enqueue adds a new element to the end of the queue, ensuring it will be processed after all elements currently in the queue. Dequeue removes and returns the element at the front, which is the oldest in the queue. In BFS, we enqueue vertices when we discover them and dequeue to process the next vertex. These operations can be implemented using a list with append for enqueue and pop(0) for dequeue in Python, but pop(0) is O(n); for efficiency, we use collections.deque with O(1) operations. Time complexity for enqueue and dequeue is O(1) with a proper queue implementation. Space complexity is O(n) for storing elements. This operation is essential for maintaining the visited set in L3, as we enqueue vertices when marking them visited. Common mistakes include using a stack (LIFO) instead of a queue, inefficient dequeue operations, and not handling empty queue conditions. The connection to L5 is that this builds on the concept of ordered data structures.

examples.input: Initial queue: [], operations: enqueue 5, enqueue 10, dequeue, enqueue 15, dequeue, dequeue
examples.trace: Step 1: Queue=[]. Step 2: Enqueue 5: queue=[5]. Step 3: Enqueue 10: queue=[5,10]. Step 4: Dequeue: remove front 5, queue=[10], output 5. Step 5: Enqueue 15: queue=[10,15]. Step 6: Dequeue: remove front 10, queue=[15], output 10. Step 7: Dequeue: remove front 15, queue=[], output 15.

examples.input: Initial queue: [2,4], operations: dequeue, enqueue 6, dequeue, enqueue 8
examples.trace: Step 1: Queue=[2,4]. Step 2: Dequeue: remove front 2, queue=[4], output 2. Step 3: Enqueue 6: queue=[4,6]. Step 4: Dequeue: remove front 4, queue=[6], output 4. Step 5: Enqueue 8: queue=[6,8]. Final queue: [6,8].

testCases.input: operations: enqueue 5, enqueue 6, enqueue 7, dequeue, dequeue
operations: enqueue 8, enqueue 9, dequeue, enqueue 10, dequeue
operations: enqueue 11, dequeue, enqueue 12, dequeue, enqueue 13
operations: enqueue 14, enqueue 15, dequeue, dequeue, dequeue (empty)
operations: enqueue 16, enqueue 17, enqueue 18, dequeue, enqueue 19
operations: enqueue 20, dequeue, enqueue 21, dequeue, enqueue 22, dequeue
operations: enqueue 23, enqueue 24, dequeue, dequeue, enqueue 25

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Graphs & Components
ladders.desc: Basic understanding of undirected graphs, vertices, edges, and connected components.
ladders.explanation: An undirected graph consists of vertices (nodes) and edges (connections). A connected component is a maximal set of vertices where every pair is connected by a path. Counting components means finding how many such separate groups exist in the graph.

examples.input: Vertices: {A, B, C}, Edges: A-B, B-C
examples.output: Graph with 3 vertices and 2 edges; adjacency: A:[B], B:[A,C], C:[B]
examples.trace: Step 1: Identify vertices: A, B, C. Step 2: List edges: edge between A and B means A connected to B and B connected to A; edge between B and C means B connected to C and C connected to B. Step 3: Build adjacency list: For A, neighbors are B → A:[B]. For B, neighbors are A and C → B:[A,C]. For C, neighbors are B → C:[B]. This represents an undirected graph.

examples.output: Graph with 4 vertices and 2 edges; adjacency: 1:[2], 2:[1], 3:[4], 4:[3]
examples.trace: Step 1: Vertices: 1,2,3,4. Step 2: Edges: 1-2 connects 1 and 2 bidirectionally; 3-4 connects 3 and 4 bidirectionally. Step 3: Adjacency list: Vertex 1: neighbor 2 → [2]. Vertex 2: neighbor 1 → [1]. Vertex 3: neighbor 4 → [4]. Vertex 4: neighbor 3 → [3]. This graph has two disconnected components.

approaches.name: Union-Find
approaches.summary: Initialize each node as its own set, union sets for each edge to merge components, and count unique roots. This approach is efficient for dynamic connectivity and avoids explicit graph traversal.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Union-Find
ladders.desc: Complete solution to count connected components in an undirected graph using Union-Find data structure.
ladders.explanation: This problem requires counting the number of connected components in an undirected graph. A connected component is a maximal set of vertices where there's a path between any two vertices. The Union-Find (Disjoint Set Union) approach provides an efficient solution by treating each vertex as its own set initially, then merging sets through edges to form components, and finally counting unique roots. The intuition is that each edge represents a connection that should merge two vertices into the same component. By processing all edges, we gradually build up the components. The algorithm initializes a parent array where each vertex is its own parent (representing separate sets). For each edge, we union the two vertices, which involves finding their roots and making one root point to the other if they're different. After processing all edges, we count how many vertices remain as their own parent (or root), which equals the number of connected components. The time complexity is O(V + E * α(V)) where α is the inverse Ackermann function (effectively constant), and space is O(V). This approach avoids explicit graph traversal like DFS/BFS and handles dynamic connectivity efficiently. Common mistakes include not implementing path compression (leading to slower finds) or union by rank (causing unbalanced trees), forgetting to handle self-loops or duplicate edges properly, and miscounting components by not checking root status correctly. The connection to L1 is that this full solution relies on the union and find operations as key sub-routines.

examples.trace: Initialize parent=[0,1,2,3,4]. Edge [0,1]: find(0)=0, find(1)=1, union(0,1) → parent[0]=1 (or parent[1]=0 with union by rank). Parent becomes [1,1,2,3,4]. Edge [1,2]: find(1)=1 (since parent[1]=1), find(2)=2, union(1,2) → parent[1]=2 (or similar). Parent becomes [1,2,2,3,4]. Edge [3,4]: find(3)=3, find(4)=4, union(3,4) → parent[3]=4. Parent becomes [1,2,2,4,4]. Count roots: indices where parent[i]==i: i=2 and i=4 → 2 components.

Initialize parent=[0,1,2,3]. Edge [0,1]: find(0)=0, find(1)=1, union(0,1) → parent[0]=1. Parent=[1,1,2,3]. Edge [2,3]: find(2)=2, find(3)=3, union(2,3) → parent[2]=3. Parent=[1,1,3,3]. Edge [1,2]: find(1)=1, find(2)=3 (since parent[2]=3, find recursively), union(1,3) → parent[1]=3. Parent=[1,3,3,3]. After path compression during finds, roots are consolidated. Count roots: only i=3 has parent[3]==3 → 1 component.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Traversal from Start
ladders.desc: Implement the union and find functions with path compression and union by rank for efficient set operations.
ladders.explanation: The union and find operations are the core of the Union-Find data structure. Find(x) returns the root (representative) of the set containing x, using path compression to flatten the tree for future efficiency. Union(x,y) merges the sets containing x and y, using union by rank to keep trees shallow. The intuition is that we maintain a forest of trees where each tree represents a set, and the root is the representative. Find works by recursively following parent pointers until reaching a node that is its own parent (the root), and during this traversal, we set each node's parent directly to the root to compress the path. Union finds the roots of x and y, and if they're different, attaches the shorter tree under the taller one to maintain balance, updating ranks accordingly. This ensures near-constant time amortized operations. Time complexity per operation is O(α(n)), space is O(n) for parent and rank arrays. This sub-routine feeds into L0 by providing the machinery to merge components and check connectivity. Common mistakes include not implementing path compression correctly (e.g., not updating parent during find), forgetting to update ranks during union, or mishandling cases where roots are equal. Real code pattern: parent = list(range(n)), rank = [0]*n; def find(x): if parent[x]!=x: parent[x]=find(parent[x]); return parent[x]; def union(x,y): rx,ry=find(x),find(y); if rx!=ry: if rank[rx]<rank[ry]: parent[rx]=ry; elif rank[rx]>rank[ry]: parent[ry]=rx; else: parent[ry]=rx; rank[rx]+=1.

examples.input: Initialize with n=4, operations: union(0,1), union(2,3), find(0), find(2), union(1,2)
examples.output: After operations, sets merged into one.
examples.trace: Start: parent=[0,1,2,3], rank=[0,0,0,0]. union(0,1): find(0)=0, find(1)=1, rank[0]=0, rank[1]=0 → parent[1]=0, rank[0]=1. Parent=[0,0,2,3], rank=[1,0,0,0]. union(2,3): find(2)=2, find(3)=3, rank[2]=0, rank[3]=0 → parent[3]=2, rank[2]=1. Parent=[0,0,2,2], rank=[1,0,1,0]. find(0): returns 0. find(2): returns 2. union(1,2): find(1)=0, find(2)=2, rank[0]=1, rank[2]=1 → parent[2]=0, rank[0]=2. Parent=[0,0,0,2], rank=[2,0,1,0]. After path compression in finds, tree flattened.

examples.input: n=5, operations: union(0,1), union(1,2), union(3,4), find(0), find(4), union(2,3)
examples.trace: Start: parent=[0,1,2,3,4], rank=[0,0,0,0,0]. union(0,1): find(0)=0, find(1)=1 → parent[1]=0, rank[0]=1. Parent=[0,0,2,3,4], rank=[1,0,0,0,0]. union(1,2): find(1)=0, find(2)=2 → parent[2]=0, rank[0]=1 (unchanged as rank[0]>rank[2]). Parent=[0,0,0,3,4], rank=[1,0,0,0,0]. union(3,4): find(3)=3, find(4)=4 → parent[4]=3, rank[3]=1. Parent=[0,0,0,3,3], rank=[1,0,0,1,0]. find(0): returns 0. find(4): returns 3. union(2,3): find(2)=0, find(3)=3, rank[0]=1, rank[3]=1 → parent[3]=0, rank[0]=2. Parent=[0,0,0,0,3], rank=[2,0,0,1,0].

testCases.input: n=4, operations: union(0,1), union(2,3), find(0), find(2)
n=5, operations: union(0,1), union(1,2), find(0), find(2)
n=6, operations: union(0,1), union(2,3), union(4,5), find(1), find(3), find(5)
n=4, operations: union(0,1), union(1,2), union(2,3), find(0), find(3)
n=7, operations: union(0,1), union(2,3), union(4,5), union(1,3), find(0), find(2)
n=8, operations: union(0,1), union(2,3), union(4,5), union(6,7), union(1,3), union(5,7), find(0), find(6)
n=9, operations: union(0,1), union(1,2), union(3,4), union(4,5), union(6,7), union(7,8), union(2,5), find(0), find(3)
n=10, operations: union(0,1), union(2,3), union(4,5), union(6,7), union(8,9), union(1,3), union(5,7), union(3,7), find(0), find(4)
n=11, operations: union(0,1), union(2,3), union(4,5), union(6,7), union(8,9), union(10,0), union(1,3), union(5,7), union(3,7), union(7,9), find(2), find(8)

ladders.title: Core Logic — Component Exploration
ladders.desc: Process each edge to union vertices, effectively building connected components without explicit traversal.
ladders.explanation: The central insight of using Union-Find for connected components is that each edge indicates two vertices should be in the same component. By iterating through all edges and performing union operations on the vertices, we gradually merge disjoint sets into larger sets representing components. Initially, each vertex is in its own set. As we process an edge (u,v), we call union(u,v), which finds the roots of u and v and merges their sets if they are different. After processing all edges, the sets correspond exactly to the connected components. The key intuition is that connectivity is transitive: if u connects to v and v connects to w, then u, v, and w are in the same component, and union operations propagate this through root pointers. This approach avoids graph traversal algorithms like DFS or BFS, making it efficient for dynamic graphs where edges are added incrementally. The time complexity is O(E * α(V)) for the union operations plus O(V) for initialization, and space is O(V). This feeds into L1 by utilizing the union and find functions to achieve the merging. Common mistakes include processing edges in the wrong order (order doesn't matter for undirected graphs), not handling duplicate edges (they can be ignored safely), or stopping early before processing all edges. Real code pattern: for u,v in edges: union(u,v). Then count components by checking how many vertices are roots.

examples.trace: Start with each vertex separate. Edge [0,1]: union(0,1) → sets {0,1} and {2}, {3}. Edge [2,3]: union(2,3) → sets {0,1}, {2,3}. Edge [1,2]: union(1,2) → find root of 1 (from set {0,1}) and root of 2 (from set {2,3}) are different, so merge sets into {0,1,2,3}. Final: one component.

Start: each vertex separate. Edge [0,1]: union(0,1) → sets {0,1}, {2}, {3}, {4}. Edge [3,4]: union(3,4) → sets {0,1}, {2}, {3,4}. Edge [1,2]: union(1,2) → merge {0,1} and {2} into {0,1,2}. Final sets: {0,1,2} and {3,4} → two components.

ladders.title: Building Block — Adjacency List
ladders.desc: Represent sets as trees where each node points to its parent, enabling efficient find and union operations.
ladders.explanation: The Disjoint Set Union data structure represents each set as a tree, where each node has a parent pointer, and the root of the tree is the representative of the set. Initially, each node is its own parent, forming a forest of singleton trees. The parent array stores these pointers: parent[i] gives the parent of node i, with root nodes satisfying parent[i] = i. This representation allows efficient union and find operations: find(x) climbs the parent pointers to the root, and union(x,y) attaches one root under another. The intuition is that trees naturally model hierarchical relationships, and by keeping trees shallow (through union by rank and path compression), operations become very fast. The space complexity is O(V) for the parent array. This building block feeds into L2 by providing the underlying data structure to merge sets via edges. Common mistakes include not initializing parent array correctly (each node should point to itself), using a different representation like adjacency lists (which is less efficient for this problem), or misunderstanding that parent pointers can form cycles only intentionally during path compression. Real code pattern: parent = list(range(n)). This simple array is the foundation for all DSU operations.

examples.trace: For n=4, we create an array of size 4. Index 0 to 3: set parent[0]=0, parent[1]=1, parent[2]=2, parent[3]=3. This represents 4 disjoint sets, each with one element as the root.

examples.input: n=5, after some unions: union(0,1), union(2,3)
examples.trace: Start: parent=[0,1,2,3,4]. union(0,1): make parent of 0 point to 1 or vice versa. Assume union makes parent[1]=0: parent=[0,0,2,3,4]. union(2,3): make parent[3]=2: parent=[0,0,2,2,4]. Now sets: {0,1} with root 0, {2,3} with root 2, {4} with root 4.

testCases.input: n=9 after union(0,1), union(2,3), union(4,5), union(6,7), union(1,3)

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Visited Tracking / Queue
ladders.desc: Implement a function to find the root (representative) of a set by following parent pointers.
ladders.explanation: The find operation is fundamental to Union-Find: given an element x, it returns the root of the set containing x. This is done by recursively following parent pointers from x until reaching a node that is its own parent (i.e., parent[x] == x), which is the root. The intuition is that the root uniquely identifies the set, and all elements in the same set will have the same root. To optimize, we use path compression: during the find, we set the parent of each visited node directly to the root, flattening the tree for future finds. This reduces time complexity to amortized O(α(n)). The operation requires O(depth) time initially, but with path compression, depth becomes very small. Space is O(1) extra per call. This basic operation feeds into L3 as it relies on the parent pointer representation. Common mistakes include not handling recursion correctly (leading to stack overflow for deep trees—use iterative approach if needed), forgetting to implement path compression, or incorrectly assuming parent pointers are always direct to the root. Real code pattern: def find(x): if parent[x] != x: parent[x] = find(parent[x]); return parent[x]. This recursively finds and compresses the path.

examples.trace: Start at node 3: parent[3]=2, so move to node 2. parent[2]=1, move to node 1. parent[1]=0, move to node 0. parent[0]=0, so root is 0. During recursion, set parent[3]=0, parent[2]=0, parent[1]=0. Final parent array becomes [0,0,0,0].
Start at node 5: parent[5]=5, so root is 5 immediately. No changes to parent array.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Graphs & Components
ladders.desc: Basic understanding of undirected graphs, vertices, edges, and connected components.
ladders.explanation: An undirected graph consists of vertices (nodes) and edges (connections). A connected component is a maximal set of vertices where every pair is connected by a path. Counting components means finding how many such separate groups exist in the graph.

examples.trace: Vertex 0 is connected to vertex 1 via the edge, so they form a set {0,1}. Vertex 2 has no connections, so it forms its own set {2}. Total components: 2.
A connects to B, B connects to C, C connects to D. By transitivity, all vertices are connected, so they form one set {A,B,C,D}. Total components: 1.
